-All scraping tools return: `{url, sections: {name: raw_text}, pages_visited, sections_requested}`
+All scraping tools return: `{url, sections: {name: raw_text}}`. When unknown section names are provided, an `unknown_sections: [name, ...]` key is also included.
 
 **Scraping Architecture (`scraping/`):**
 
-- `fields.py` - `PersonScrapingFields` and `CompanyScrapingFields` Flag enums
+- `fields.py` - `PERSON_SECTIONS` and `COMPANY_SECTIONS` config dicts mapping section name to `(url_suffix, is_overlay)`
 - `extractor.py` - `LinkedInExtractor` class using navigate-scroll-innerText pattern
-- **One flag = one navigation.** Each `PersonScrapingFields` / `CompanyScrapingFields` flag must map to exactly one page navigation. Never combine multiple URLs behind a single flag.
+- **One section = one navigation.** Each entry in `PERSON_SECTIONS` / `COMPANY_SECTIONS` maps to exactly one page navigation. Never combine multiple URLs behind a single section.
 
 **Core Subpackage (`core/`):**
 
@@ -138,6 +138,16 @@ All scraping tools return: `{url, sections: {name: raw_text}, pages_visited, sec
   8. Manually review the PR and merge it if it's approved. Do not squash the commits.
   9. Delete the branch after the PR is merged.
 
+## PR Reviews
+
+Greptile posts initial reviews as PR review comments, but follow-ups as **issue comments**. Always check both. To trigger a re-review, comment `@greptileai review` on the PR.
+
+```bash
+gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/reviews    # initial reviews
+gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments   # inline comments
+gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{pr}/comments   # follow-up reviews
+```
+
 ## btca
 
 When you need up-to-date information about technologies used in this project, use btca to query source repositories directly.

 uv run pytest --cov                        # Run tests with coverage
 ```
 
-## Architecture: One Flag = One Navigation
+## Architecture: One Section = One Navigation
 
-The scraping engine is built around a **one-flag-one-navigation** design. Understanding this is key to contributing effectively.
+The scraping engine is built around a **one-section-one-navigation** design. Understanding this is key to contributing effectively.
 
 ### Why This Design?
 
-AI assistants (LLMs) call our MCP tools. Each LinkedIn page navigation takes time and risks rate limits. By mapping each `Flag` to exactly one URL, the LLM can request only the sections it needs — skipping unnecessary navigations while still capturing all available info from each visited page via `innerText` extraction.
+AI assistants (LLMs) call our MCP tools. Each LinkedIn page navigation takes time and risks rate limits. By mapping each section to exactly one URL, the LLM can request only the sections it needs — skipping unnecessary navigations while still capturing all available info from each visited page via `innerText` extraction.
 
 ### How It Works
 
-**Flag enums** (`scraping/fields.py`) define which pages exist:
+**Section config dicts** (`scraping/fields.py`) define which pages exist:
 
 ```python
-class PersonScrapingFields(Flag):
-    BASIC_INFO = auto()  # /in/{username}/
-    EXPERIENCE = auto()  # /in/{username}/details/experience/
-    CONTACT_INFO = auto()  # /in/{username}/overlay/contact-info/
-    LANGUAGES = auto()  # /in/{username}/details/languages/
-    # ...
-```
-
-**Section maps** connect user-facing names to flags:
-
-```python
-PERSON_SECTION_MAP = {
-    "experience": PersonScrapingFields.EXPERIENCE,
-    "contact_info": PersonScrapingFields.CONTACT_INFO,
+# Maps section name -> (url_suffix, is_overlay)
+PERSON_SECTIONS: dict[str, tuple[str, bool]] = {
+    "main_profile": ("/", False),
+    "experience": ("/details/experience/", False),
+    "contact_info": ("/overlay/contact-info/", True),
+    "languages": ("/details/languages/", False),
     # ...
 }
 ```
 
-**Page maps** (`scraping/extractor.py`) wire flags to URLs:
+The `is_overlay` boolean distinguishes modal overlays (like contact info) from full page navigations — overlays use a different extraction method that reads from the `<dialog>` element.
+
+The extractor iterates the config dict directly, checking which sections the caller requested:
 
 ```python
-# (flag, section_name, url_suffix, is_overlay)
-page_map = [
-    (PersonScrapingFields.BASIC_INFO, "main_profile", "/", False),
-    (PersonScrapingFields.EXPERIENCE, "experience", "/details/experience/", False),
-    (PersonScrapingFields.CONTACT_INFO, "contact_info", "/overlay/contact-info/", True),
-    # ...
-]
+for section_name, (suffix, is_overlay) in PERSON_SECTIONS.items():
+    if section_name not in requested:
+        continue
+    # navigate and extract...
 ```
 
-The `is_overlay` boolean distinguishes modal overlays (like contact info) from full page navigations — overlays use a different extraction method that reads from the `<dialog>` element.
-
 **Return format** — all scraping tools return:
 
 ```python
-{"url": str, "sections": {name: raw_text}, "pages_visited": list, "sections_requested": list}
+{"url": str, "sections": {name: raw_text}}
+# When unknown section names are provided:
+{"url": str, "sections": {name: raw_text}, "unknown_sections": [name, ...]}
 ```

problem_statement:
refactor(scraping): simplify return format and remove Flag enum indirection
## Summary

The scraping architecture has unnecessary indirection and redundant return fields that can be simplified.

## Redundant return fields

All scraping tools return `{url, sections, pages_visited, sections_requested}`, but:

- **`sections_requested`** — the agent already knows what it requested in the same chat turn. Partial failures are detectable by comparing the request against `sections` keys.
- **`pages_visited`** — always the same URLs implied by the requested flags. Redundant.

Simplify to `{url, sections}`.

## Flag enum indirection

Current flow converts strings through an unnecessary intermediate:

1. MCP tool receives `sections: str` → `"experience,education"`
2. `parse_person_sections()` converts strings → `Flag` enum via `SECTION_MAP`
3. `scrape_person()` converts flags back to strings/URLs via `page_map` + `reverse_map`

Composite flags (`ALL`, `ESSENTIAL`) were likely the original motivation for `Flag` but were never implemented — and aren't needed since LLMs construct explicit section lists.

## Proposed change

Collapse Flag enum, SECTION_MAP, and page_map into one config dict per entity:

```python
PERSON_SECTIONS = {
    "main_profile": ("/", False),
    "experience": ("/details/experience/", False),
    "contact_info": ("/overlay/contact-info/", True),
    # ...
}
```

`scrape_person` takes `requested: set[str]` and iterates directly. Removes `PersonScrapingFields`, `CompanyScrapingFields`, `PERSON_SECTION_MAP`, `COMPANY_SECTION_MAP`, `parse_person_sections()`, `parse_company_sections()`, and the reverse_map logic.

+"""Dependency injection factories for MCP tools."""
+
+from linkedin_mcp_server.drivers.browser import (
+    ensure_authenticated,
+    get_or_create_browser,
+)
+from linkedin_mcp_server.error_handler import raise_tool_error
+from linkedin_mcp_server.scraping import LinkedInExtractor
+
+
+async def get_extractor() -> LinkedInExtractor:
+    """Acquire the singleton browser, authenticate, and return a ready extractor.
+
+    Known LinkedIn exceptions are converted to structured ToolError responses
+    via raise_tool_error(); unexpected exceptions propagate as-is.
+    """
+    try:
+        browser = await get_or_create_browser()
+        await ensure_authenticated()
+        return LinkedInExtractor(browser.page)
+    except Exception as e:
+        raise_tool_error(e, "get_extractor")  # NoReturn

problem_statement:
[FEATURE] Use Depends() to inject browser/extractor into tools
## Feature description
Use FastMCP 3.x dependency injection (`Depends()`) to eliminate the repeated `ensure_authenticated()` + `get_or_create_browser()` + `LinkedInExtractor(...)` boilerplate in every tool function.

Currently every tool manually calls:
```python
await ensure_authenticated()
browser = await get_or_create_browser()
extractor = LinkedInExtractor(browser.page)
```

With `Depends()`, this becomes a single injected parameter hidden from the MCP tool schema.

## Use case
- Removes ~5 lines of identical boilerplate from every tool
- Browser/extractor setup is defined once, reused everywhere
- Injected dependencies don't appear in the tool's MCP schema (invisible to LLM clients)
- Easier to test — can mock the dependency function

## Suggested implementation
1. Create a dependency function (e.g., `get_extractor()`) that handles auth + browser + extractor setup
2. Add `extractor=Depends(get_extractor)` parameter to each tool function
3. Remove manual auth/browser/extractor setup from tool bodies

```python
from fastmcp.dependencies import Depends

async def get_extractor():
    await ensure_authenticated()
    browser = await get_or_create_browser()
    return LinkedInExtractor(browser.page)

@mcp.tool(timeout=30.0)
async def get_person_profile(url: str, ctx: Context, extractor=Depends(get_extractor)):
    result = await extractor.scrape_person(url)
    return result
```

## Additional context
- Requires FastMCP >=3.0.0
- Depends on PR #166 (fastmcp upgrade) being merged first
- Should be stacked after the ToolError PR (both touch all tool files)
- Ref: https://gofastmcp.com/servers/tools#dependencies

problem_statement:
[FEATURE] Add per-tool timeouts to prevent hanging scrapes
## Feature description
Add FastMCP 3.x `timeout` parameter to all scraping tools to prevent indefinite hangs when LinkedIn pages fail to load or respond.

## Use case
- Scraping tools can hang indefinitely on slow/broken pages
- Tool timeouts automatically return an MCP error to the client after the deadline
- No manual timeout logic needed in tool code
- Improves reliability for all MCP clients

## Suggested implementation
Add `timeout=` to each `@mcp.tool()` decorator:

```python
@mcp.tool(timeout=30.0)  # 30 second timeout
async def get_person_profile(...):
    ...
```

Suggested timeouts:
- `get_person_profile`: 60s (multiple page navigations for sections)
- `get_company_profile`: 60s (multiple page navigations)
- `get_company_posts`: 45s
- `get_job_details`: 30s
- `search_jobs`: 45s
- `search_people`: 45s
- `close_session`: 10s

## Additional context
- Requires FastMCP >=3.0.0
- Depends on PR #166 (fastmcp upgrade) being merged first
- Small, independent PR — can be done in parallel with other improvements
- Ref: https://gofastmcp.com/servers/tools#timeout

 - `extractor.py` - `LinkedInExtractor` class using navigate-scroll-innerText pattern
 - **One section = one navigation.** Each entry in `PERSON_SECTIONS` / `COMPANY_SECTIONS` maps to exactly one page navigation. Never combine multiple URLs behind a single section.
+- **Minimize DOM dependence.** Prefer innerText and URL navigation over DOM selectors. When DOM access is unavoidable (e.g. extracting `href` attributes, finding scrollable containers), use minimal generic selectors (`a[href*="/jobs/view/"]`) — never class names tied to LinkedIn's layout.

index 9d1d8107..14ddca04 100644
--- a/CONTRIBUTING.md
+++ b/CONTRIBUTING.md
@@ -124,6 +124,16 @@ When adding an entirely new MCP tool (e.g., `search_companies`):
 4. Open a PR — AI agents review first, then manual review
 5. Don't squash commits on merge
 
+## Scraping Philosophy: Minimize DOM Dependence
+
+This project favours **innerText extraction and URL navigation** over DOM selectors. LinkedIn's markup changes frequently — class names, `data-` attributes, and component structure are unstable. Our scraping engine is deliberately built to survive those changes:
+
+- **Prefer `innerText`** over `querySelector` / DOM walking for data extraction.
+- **Prefer URL navigation** (e.g. `/details/experience/`) over clicking UI elements.
+- **When DOM access is unavoidable** (e.g. extracting `href` attributes that don't appear in innerText, finding a scrollable container), keep selectors minimal and generic. Favour tag + attribute patterns (`a[href*="/jobs/view/"]`) over class names (`.jobs-search-results-list`).
+- **Never scope queries to layout-specific containers** like `.jobs-search-results-list` — these break silently when LinkedIn redesigns. Use `main` as the broadest acceptable scope.
+- **Document any DOM dependency** with a comment explaining why innerText/URL navigation isn't sufficient.
+
 ## Code Style
 
 - **Commits:** conventional commits — `type(scope): subject` (see [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) for details)

+async def scroll_job_sidebar(
+    page: Page, pause_time: float = 1.0, max_scrolls: int = 10
+) -> None:
+    """Scroll the job search sidebar to load all job cards.
+
+    LinkedIn renders job search results in a scrollable sidebar container,
+    not the main page body. This function finds that container by locating
+    a job card link and walking up to its scrollable ancestor, then scrolls
+    it iteratively until no new content loads.
+
+    Args:
+        page: Patchright page object
+        pause_time: Time to pause between scrolls (seconds)
+        max_scrolls: Maximum number of scroll attempts
+    """
+    # Wait for at least one job card link to render before scrolling
+    try:
+        await page.wait_for_selector('a[href*="/jobs/view/"]', timeout=5000)
+    except PlaywrightTimeoutError:
+        logger.debug("No job card links found, skipping sidebar scroll")
+        return
+
+    scrolled = await page.evaluate(
+        """async ({pauseTime, maxScrolls}) => {
+            const link = document.querySelector('a[href*="/jobs/view/"]');
+            if (!link) return -2;
+
+            let container = link.parentElement;
+            while (container && container !== document.body) {
+                const style = window.getComputedStyle(container);
+                const overflowY = style.overflowY;
+                if ((overflowY === 'auto' || overflowY === 'scroll')
+                    && container.scrollHeight > container.clientHeight) {
+                    break;
+                }
+                container = container.parentElement;
+            }
+
+            if (!container || container === document.body) {
+                return -1;
+            }
+
+            let scrollCount = 0;
+            for (let i = 0; i < maxScrolls; i++) {
+                const prevHeight = container.scrollHeight;
+                container.scrollTop = container.scrollHeight;
+                await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, pauseTime * 1000));
+                if (container.scrollHeight === prevHeight) break;
+                scrollCount++;
+            }
+            return scrollCount;
+        }""",
+        {"pauseTime": pause_time, "maxScrolls": max_scrolls},
+    )
+    if scrolled == -2:
+        logger.debug("Job card link disappeared before evaluate, skipping scroll")
+    elif scrolled == -1:
+        logger.debug("No scrollable container found for job sidebar")
+    elif scrolled:
+        logger.debug("Scrolled job sidebar %d times", scrolled)
+    else:
+        logger.debug("Job sidebar container found but no new content loaded")
+

problem_statement:
get_person_profile: posts section returns only tab headers, no actual post content
## Bug Description

The `posts` section added in commit [a8d1fb8](https://github.com/stickerdaniel/linkedin-mcp-server/commit/a8d1fb83d5d6627c5a5a1529ad87fae5c90ac71a) correctly navigates to `/in/{username}/recent-activity/all/` but only returns tab header text ("All activity\nPosts\nComments\nImages\nReactions") instead of actual post content.

## Root Cause

Two issues in `_extract_page_once()`:

1. **Insufficient scrolling** — `max_scrolls=5` with `pause_time=0.5` is not enough for the activity feed. LinkedIn lazy-loads posts and needs more scroll iterations with longer pauses to trigger content rendering.

2. **Noise stripping removes post content** — `strip_linkedin_noise()` matches patterns inside post text (e.g., "About\nAccessibility" in footer-like content within posts) and truncates everything from that point.

## Fix

Adding activity page detection to `_extract_page_once()` resolves both issues:

```python
async def _extract_page_once(self, url: str) -> str:
    is_activity_page = "/recent-activity/" in url
    
    # ... existing code ...
    
    # Wait for actual post elements to load
    if is_activity_page:
        try:
            await self._page.wait_for_selector(
                ".feed-shared-update-v2, [data-urn]",
                timeout=8000,
            )
        except PlaywrightTimeoutError:
            pass
    
    # More scrolls for activity pages
    if is_activity_page:
        await scroll_to_bottom(self._page, pause_time=1.5, max_scrolls=12)
    else:
        await scroll_to_bottom(self._page, pause_time=0.5, max_scrolls=5)
    
    # ... extract raw text ...
    
    # Skip noise stripping for activity pages
    if is_activity_page:
        return raw.strip()
    
    # ... existing noise stripping for other pages ...
```

## Result

With this patch applied locally, `get_person_profile` with `sections="posts"` successfully returns full post content (tested with 52 posts loaded from a single profile).

## Environment

- linkedin-scraper-mcp installed from `main` branch (post-commit a8d1fb8)
- macOS, Patchright/Chromium, streamable-http transport
- Python 3.14

problem_statement:
[BUG] search_people intermittently returns empty results (content not loaded before extraction)
## Installation Method

- [X] uvx setup (v4.4.0)

## When does the error occur?

- [X] During tool call: `search_people`

## Error Description

`search_people` returns `{"sections": {}}` for roughly 40% of searches, even though the browser correctly renders the results.

### Evidence

I investigated using the trace screenshots in `~/.linkedin-mcp/trace-runs/`:

- **"before-goto" screenshots** show the search results page fully populated (names, connection degree, mutual connections all visible)
- **"after-goto" screenshots** show a loading placeholder page with gray bars and no text content

The extractor reads `innerText` during this loading state, getting empty text.

### Root cause

In `_extract_page_once()`, `wait_for_selector("main")` returns immediately because the `<main>` element exists as a placeholder container. There is no check for actual content in the search results path.

This is the same issue that was fixed for activity feed pages in #203 — activity feeds got a `wait_for_function` check for `main.innerText.length > 200`, but search results pages were not included.

### Suggested fix

Same pattern as #203 — add `wait_for_function` for `/search/results/` URLs:

```python
is_search = "/search/results/" in url
if is_search:
    try:
        await self._page.wait_for_function(
            """() => {
                const main = document.querySelector('main');
                if (!main) return false;
                return main.innerText.length > 100;
            }""",
            timeout=10000,
        )

Applied the fix locally and tested with 8 searches that previously failed intermittently. All 8 returned results successfully (100% vs ~60% before).

problem_statement:
[BUG] --chrome-path flag is ignored during --login flow
## Setup

uvx / uv run on macOS (ARM), any MCP client

## What Happened

When running `--login` with `--chrome-path "/Applications/Brave Browser.app/Contents/MacOS/Brave Browser"`, the custom browser path is ignored. The server still tries to launch Patchright's bundled Chromium at `~/.linkedin-mcp/patchright-browsers/chromium-1208/chrome-mac-arm64/` instead of the specified executable.

The `--chrome-path` flag works correctly during normal server runtime (the `drivers/browser.py:_launch_options()` path), but the `--login` flow in `setup.py:interactive_login()` creates a `BrowserManager` without passing `executable_path`.

**Expected:** `--chrome-path` should be respected in both the server runtime and the `--login` flow.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run: `uv run -m linkedin_mcp_server --login --chrome-path "/Applications/Brave Browser.app/Contents/MacOS/Brave Browser"`
2. Observe error about missing Patchright bundled Chromium instead of launching Brave

## Root Cause

`setup.py:interactive_login()` constructs `BrowserManager(user_data_dir=..., headless=False)` without reading `config.browser.chrome_path` or passing it as `executable_path`. The server runtime path in `drivers/browser.py:_launch_options()` correctly does this at line 189-190, but the login codepath was never wired up.

problem_statement:
[BUG] send_message: hardcoded 5s timeout causes composer_unavailable on LinkedIn SPA
## Description

`send_message` consistently returns `composer_unavailable` because the compose overlay URL (`/messaging/compose/?recipient=...`) doesn't render within the hardcoded 5000ms timeout. LinkedIn's SPA needs longer to hydrate the compose modal.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Call `send_message` with a valid `linkedin_username` and `profile_urn` for a 1st-degree connection with an existing conversation
2. The tool navigates to `/messaging/compose/?recipient={urn}`
3. Returns `{"status": "composer_unavailable", "recipient_selected": false, "sent": false}`

## Root Cause

The timeouts in the `send_message` flow are hardcoded to 5000ms in the method body (`extractor.py` ~line 2296), not derived from `BrowserConfig.default_timeout`. This means the `--timeout` CLI flag has no effect on this flow.

The compose overlay URL triggers a full SPA load -- screenshots from the trace show the page stuck on the LinkedIn loading spinner at the point the timeout fires (body_length: 21, just "0 notifications total").

## Evidence from Trace

```
Step 13: navigate to /messaging/compose/?profileUrn=...&recipient=...
  -> body_length: 21, body_marker: "0 notifications total"
  -> screenshot: LinkedIn loading spinner only

Step 17: navigate to /messaging/compose/?recipient=...
  -> body_length: 21, body_marker: "0 notifications total"
  -> screenshot: LinkedIn loading spinner only
```

Meanwhile, navigating to the thread URL directly (`/messaging/thread/{threadId}/`) renders the full conversation with a working compose box in the same browser session.

## Suggested Fix

Two options (not mutually exclusive):

1. **Respect the global timeout**: Have `_resolve_message_compose_box()` and `_wait_for_message_surface()` use `BrowserConfig.default_timeout` instead of hardcoded 5000ms, so users can tune it with `--timeout`.

2. **Use thread URL for existing conversations**: When an existing conversation exists (detectable from the inbox), navigate to `/messaging/thread/{threadId}/` and type in the message box there, instead of using the compose overlay URL. This is significantly more reliable -- the thread page renders the compose box inline without needing a separate SPA modal.

## Environment

- Version: 4.8.0 (via `uvx linkedin-scraper-mcp`)
- Runtime: linux-amd64 (WSL2)
- Transport: stdio
- Browser: headless Chromium (patchright)
[BUG] Respect --timeout CLI flag across all scraping methods
### Feature Description

After fixing the messaging-specific hardcoded timeouts (#296), there are still 10 remaining `timeout=5000` values in non-messaging methods in `extractor.py`. These bypass `BrowserConfig.default_timeout` the same way the messaging ones did, making the `--timeout` CLI flag ineffective for:

- Profile/page scraping (lines 543, 699)
- Connection flow (lines 1026, 1040, 1064, 1123)
- Modal detection (line 814)
- Sidebar profiles (line 1233)
- Conversation thread resolution (line 1629)
- Job search (line 1841)

### Use Case

Users on slower environments (WSL2, CI, Docker) or flaky networks often need longer timeouts. The `--timeout` flag exists specifically for this, but these hardcoded values override it silently. This is the same class of bug as #296, just across different scraping flows.

### Suggested Approach

Same pattern used in #296 fix: remove explicit `timeout=5000` from Playwright calls so they fall through to the page's `set_default_timeout()` value (which is set from `BrowserConfig.default_timeout`). Short probe timeouts (like `_locator_is_visible` at 2000ms) should be kept since they serve a different purpose.

@@ -734,6 +734,27 @@ async def _extract_page_once(
             except PlaywrightTimeoutError:
                 logger.debug("Search results content did not appear on %s", url)
 
+        # Profile detail pages (/details/experience/, /details/education/, etc.)
+        # initially render sidebar recommendations into <main> while the section
+        # panel loads asynchronously. Wait until the panel replaces the sidebar.
+        # The sidebar placeholder starts with "Load more" or "More profiles for you".
+        is_details = "/details/" in url
+        if is_details:
+            try:
+                await self._page.wait_for_function(
+                    """() => {
+                        const main = document.querySelector('main');
+                        if (!main) return false;
+                        const text = main.innerText.trimStart();
+                        return !text.startsWith('Load more')
+                            && !text.startsWith('More profiles for you')
+                            && !text.startsWith('Explore premium profiles');
+                    }""",
+                    timeout=10000,
+                )
+            except PlaywrightTimeoutError:
+                logger.debug("Detail section content did not appear on %s", url)
+
         # Scroll to trigger lazy loading
         if is_activity:
             await scroll_to_bottom(self._page, pause_time=1.0, max_scrolls=10)

problem_statement:
[FEATURE] Add skills and projects sections to get_person_profile
### Feature Description

Add `skills` and `projects` as selectable sections for the `get_person_profile` tool. LinkedIn exposes these at `/details/skills/` and `/details/projects/` respectively, following the same URL pattern as existing sections (experience, education, certifications, etc.).

### Use Case

Community request from [discussion #13](https://github.com/stickerdaniel/linkedin-mcp-server/discussions/13#discussioncomment-16547342). Users want to retrieve skills and projects data alongside other profile sections.

### Suggested Approach

Add two entries to `PERSON_SECTIONS` in `scraping/fields.py` following the existing `/details/<section>/` pattern. Update docstrings, tests, README, manifest, and docs.
